import { Meta } from '@storybook/blocks';

<Meta title="Introduction/Storybook Guide" />

# Storybook Guide

This guide covers how the YaleSites design system Storybook is organized, what the conventions are, and what you need to do when adding or updating components.

## Who This Is For

This Storybook serves three audiences:

- **YaleSites developers** — the primary audience. Use this as the source of truth for component behavior, design options, and documentation conventions when building or maintaining the design system.
- **External developers** — contributors or teams building on top of the YaleSites design system. Use this to understand how components work, what options are available, and how to contribute back correctly.
- **Yale brand reference** — designers, communicators, and Yale staff who need to understand what the YaleSites visual language looks like. The token and color sections in particular document Yale's web brand.

---

## How the Sidebar Is Organized

The sidebar follows an atomic design hierarchy:

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Section</th>
      <th>What lives here</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>
        <strong>Introduction</strong>
      </td>
      <td>Orientation pages — Welcome, Theme System, this guide</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        <strong>Tokens</strong>
      </td>
      <td>
        Design tokens: color palettes, typography, spacing, effects, breakpoints
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        <strong>Atoms</strong>
      </td>
      <td>
        The smallest building blocks: buttons, images, dividers, lists,
        typography, read time, link skip
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        <strong>Molecules</strong>
      </td>
      <td>
        Components composed of atoms: callouts, cards, meta types, quotes,
        modals
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        <strong>Organisms</strong>
      </td>
      <td>
        Full page sections composed of molecules: banners, content spotlights,
        tabs, header, footer, calendar, card collections
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        <strong>Templates</strong>
      </td>
      <td>
        Structural scaffolding with no visual identity of their own: block
        wrapper, component wrapper, layout, page layouts
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        <strong>Page Examples</strong>
      </td>
      <td>Real-world page examples for reference</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

When adding a new component, place it in the tier that matches its complexity and purpose:

- A single UI element with no moving parts is an **atom**.
- A component made of atoms working together to form a meaningful UI unit is a **molecule**.
- A self-contained page section composed of molecules — something that spans significant width or has its own structural presence — is an **organism**.
- A layout shell or Drupal wiring component with no visual identity of its own is a **template**.

---

## Story Files vs. MDX Pages

Every component can have two types of documentation:

### Story files (`*.stories.js`)

Story files define the interactive previews. They export named stories that Storybook renders in Canvas blocks. Use story files to:

- Define `argTypes` and `args` for interactive controls
- Export named variants of a component (e.g. `Default`, `WithImage`, `DarkTheme`)
- Set up visual regression exports (see [Visreg Stories](#visreg-stories) below)

### MDX pages (`*.mdx`)

MDX pages are the documentation layer. They wrap stories with written context. Use MDX pages to:

- Explain what a component is and when to use it
- Document design decisions, constraints, and accessibility notes
- Embed Canvas blocks for component previews
- Reference related components or token pages

**Every component should have both.** The story file handles interactivity; the MDX page handles explanation.

---

## The Canvas Rule

> **Canvas blocks are for front-end component previews only. All explanatory text, headings, tables, and reference content belong in MDX.**

This is the most important convention in this Storybook.

**Do this:**

```mdx
## Color Variations

The divider supports three line colors, shown below on themed backgrounds.

<Canvas of={DividerStories.DividersColor} />
```

**Not this (text baked into the story):**

```js
export const DividersColor = () => `
  <h2>Color Variations</h2>
  <p>The divider supports three line colors...</p>
  ${dividerTwig(...)}
`;
```

When story exports contain `<h2>`, `<p>`, or other document-level text, that text should be moved into the MDX page and the Canvas should contain only the rendered component.

---

## Canvas Width

By default, Canvas blocks render at a wide width suitable for full-bleed components. For smaller, self-contained components, wrap the Canvas in `sb-contained-canvas` to constrain it:

```mdx
<div className="sb-contained-canvas">
  <Canvas of={ComponentStories.Default} />
</div>
```

Use `sb-contained-canvas` for: atoms, small molecules, reference tables, token swatches.

Use the default wide canvas for: banners, full-width layouts, organisms, any component that needs horizontal space to read correctly.

---

## Tags

Tags control how stories appear in the sidebar and which tooling picks them up.

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Tag</th>
      <th>What it does</th>
      <th>When to use it</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>
        <code>'!dev'</code>
      </td>
      <td>Hides the story from the sidebar</td>
      <td>
        Apply to any story whose content is fully covered by an MDX docs page
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        <code>'visreg'</code>
      </td>
      <td>
        Marks the story for visual regression testing; hidden by default, shown
        via the Visreg toolbar toggle
      </td>
      <td>
        Apply to all <code>*.visreg.stories.js</code> exports
      </td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

Apply tags after the export:

```js
export const MyStory = () => myTwig(data);
MyStory.tags = ['!dev'];
```

Or at the default export level to apply to all stories in the file:

```js
export default {
  title: 'Atoms/MyComponent',
  tags: ['!dev'],
};
```

---

## Naming and Sidebar Order

Story titles follow the pattern `Tier/ComponentName` or `Tier/Group/ComponentName`:

```js
export default {
  title: 'Molecules/Banner/Action Banner',
};
```

Sidebar ordering within a section is alphabetical by default. To override the order for a specific group, add an entry to the `storySort.order` array in `.storybook/preview.js`:

```js
order: [
  'Introduction',
  ['Welcome', 'Theme System', 'Storybook Guide'],
  'Tokens',
  ['Colors', ['Colors', 'Color Palettes (Theme)', 'Theme Sandbox', 'Theming Reference']],
  ...
]
```

MDX pages that are standalone (not tied to a stories file) use `<Meta title="..." />`. MDX pages that are attached to a stories file use `<Meta of={StoriesObject} name="Overview" />` — the `name` field sets the tab label in the sidebar. Always use `name="Overview"` rather than `name="Docs"`.

---

## The Theme System in Storybook

The toolbar at the top of Storybook has two controls that affect how components render:

**Site: Global Theme (lever)** — switches the active color palette site-wide. All components in the Canvas update to reflect the selected theme. When building or reviewing a component, check it in multiple global themes to make sure it responds correctly.

**Visreg toggle** — shows or hides visual regression stories in the sidebar (hidden by default). Use this when you need to review or update visreg exports.

Both controls persist across page navigation via `localStorage`.

---

## Visreg Stories

Visual regression stories (`*.visreg.stories.js`) are a snapshot reference for QA during releases. They render fixed, non-interactive versions of components in their key states and variants so changes can be visually diffed.

### When you must add a visreg story

Add or update a visreg story whenever you:

- **Add a new component** — create a `component-name.visreg.stories.js` file with all meaningful variants
- **Add a new design option or color** — add the new option as a story export in the existing visreg file
- **Add a new layout variation or modifier** — add it to the visreg file
- **Change the visual output of an existing story** — update the visreg export to match

If a new design option ships without a visreg story, it won't be caught during release QA. Every variation that can appear on a live site should have a corresponding visreg entry.

### Visreg story conventions

```js
// component-name.visreg.stories.js
export default {
  title: 'Atoms/MyComponent',
  tags: ['visreg'],
};

export const Default = () => myTwig({ ...defaultData });
export const WithDarkBackground = () =>
  myTwig({ ...defaultData, theme: 'one' });
```

Tag the default export with `'visreg'` — not `'!dev'`. This keeps them hidden from the regular sidebar but visible when the Visreg toggle is on.

---

## Adding a New Component

### 1. Choose the right tier

- **Atom** — a single, indivisible UI element (button, icon, divider, heading, read time)
- **Molecule** — a component made of atoms working together (callout, card, meta types, modal)
- **Organism** — a self-contained page section with significant structural presence (banner, content spotlight, tabs, site header, footer, card collection)
- **Template** — a layout shell or Drupal wiring component with no visual identity of its own (block wrapper, component wrapper, layout)

### 2. Create the required files

At minimum, a new component needs:

```
components/[tier]/[component-name]/
  yds-[component-name].twig            ← Twig template
  _yds-[component-name].scss           ← Styles
  [component-name].stories.js          ← Story file with interactive controls
  [component-name].mdx                 ← Docs page
  [component-name].visreg.stories.js   ← Visual regression stories
```

Where `[tier]` is one of: `01-atoms`, `02-molecules`, `03-organisms`, `04-page-layouts` (for templates).

### 3. Minimum viable MDX page

Every component docs page should cover:

- **What it is** — one or two sentences describing the component and its purpose
- **Interactive Preview** — a Canvas block with Controls so developers can explore it
- **When to use it** — guidance on appropriate use cases
- **Design options** — document every dial, slot, or theme option available
- **Accessibility notes** — any constraints or requirements specific to this component

```mdx
# Component Name

Brief description of what this component does and when to use it.

## Interactive Preview

<Canvas of={ComponentStories.Default} />
<Controls of={ComponentStories.Default} />

## Design Options

Document available theme dials, layout variants, etc.

## Accessibility

Any accessibility requirements or constraints.
```

### 4. Add to the Theming Reference

If the new component supports color theming (a dial or slot), add it to the [Theming Reference](?path=/docs/tokens-colors-theming-reference--docs) table.

---

## Quick Reference

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Rule</th>
      <th>Detail</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>Canvas = component previews only</td>
      <td>Move all text, headings, and tables to MDX</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Wide canvas by default</td>
      <td>
        Use <code>sb-contained-canvas</code> for small/contained components
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        Tag <code>!dev</code> when MDX covers the story
      </td>
      <td>Hides raw story from sidebar</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        Tag <code>visreg</code> for regression stories
      </td>
      <td>Keeps them hidden unless toggled on</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>New option or variation = new visreg entry</td>
      <td>Required for release QA coverage</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>All component tiers need a visreg file</td>
      <td>Atoms, molecules, organisms, and templates alike</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>
